Description: Create a single operational dashboard for application health, pytest status, quality trends, and release readiness.
Unify health, logs, traces, metrics, and alerts into one grid for engineering and operations.
Standardize full-suite pytest execution, reporting artifacts, flaky detection, coverage gates, and security checks.
Provide mandatory release checklist, risk scoring, and evidence collection for secure SaaS deployments.

Summary: Single dashboard shell with grid layout
Description: As an engineering manager I can open one dashboard and see all quality and runtime status panels in a structured grid. AC: responsive grid with status cards for app health, tests, monitoring, alerts, and deployments.
As an operator I can see backend, dashboard, and mobile runtime status. AC: panel shows up/down, port, uptime, and last heartbeat for each service.
As a developer I can see latest pytest totals and outcome. AC: panel shows passed, failed, skipped, duration, and timestamp from latest run artifact.
As QA I can inspect test module-level results quickly. AC: table shows file, total tests, failed tests, duration, and links to failure snippets.
As a lead I can track pass rate trend over time. AC: chart plots pass rate, runtime, and failure count for last N runs.
As a release owner I can verify minimum test coverage gate. AC: dashboard shows line coverage and indicates gate pass/fail against threshold.
As SRE I can view key service metrics directly in the same grid. AC: include request rate, error rate, latency P95/P99, and saturation indicators.

Summary: Log stream panel with severity filters
Description: As an on-call engineer I can view recent logs by severity and service. AC: filters for service, level, timeframe; errors highlighted.
As a backend engineer I can inspect slow endpoints and trace IDs. AC: panel lists slowest requests with endpoint, duration, trace id, and drill-down link.
As on-call I can see active alerts and their state. AC: panel shows triggered alerts, severity, acknowledge state, and time since trigger.

Summary: Tenant isolation guardrails for observability data
Description: As a SaaS owner I prevent cross-tenant leakage in all quality and observability endpoints. AC: tenant-scoped filters enforced; cross-tenant access returns 404/401 and audited.
As a developer I can run all tests with one command. AC: script executes full pytest suite with consistent options and writes machine-readable artifacts.
As CI I can consume standardized test artifacts. AC: full suite produces junit.xml and summary.json in deterministic paths.
As QA I can read concise failure summaries without opening full logs. AC: extractor stores top failure traces with file and test id metadata.

Summary: Flaky test detection and quarantine workflow
Description: As test owner I can identify unstable tests and quarantine safely. AC: repeated-failure detection flags flaky tests and emits actionable list.
As engineering I can reduce total test runtime. AC: optional parallel profile with stable output and no ordering-related regressions.
As security team I can run auth and tenant-isolation checks before release. AC: dedicated lane for auth, RBAC, tenant boundary, and secrets validation tests.
As release manager I can verify mandatory release criteria in one place. AC: checklist includes tests, coverage, vulnerabilities, migrations, and rollback readiness.
As leadership I can see a single release risk score. AC: score derived from failures, flaky count, latency regressions, and open critical alerts.
As compliance owner I can export release evidence. AC: generate downloadable bundle with test reports, observability snapshots, and checklist status.
As admin I can control who sees operational quality data. AC: RBAC roles for viewer/editor/admin enforced across all quality endpoints and panels.
As platform owner I enforce retention and redaction for logs and test artifacts. AC: retention window configurable and sensitive tokens/PII redacted from UI and exports.
